16: The Nazareth synagogue scene is both Luke's inauguration narrative and his programmatic Christological-social summary. Synagogue worship in the first century included Torah readings, Prophets readings (haftarah), prayer, and exposition; there was no fixed lectionary for the Prophets readings, allowing Jesus to select Isa 61:1-2. The Jubilee language of Isa 61 (release to captives, year of the Lord's favor = the year of Jubilee from Lev 25) would have evoked economic liberation for the impoverished Galilean audience. The crowd's initial favorable response (v.22) followed by fury (v.28) when Jesus cites the Gentile precedents (Zarephath, Naaman) is the reception pattern that the whole Gospel will repeat: initial receptivity followed by rejection at the universal-mission implication.
1: The temptation narrative occurs immediately after Jesus' baptism and the Spirit's descent (3:21-22). Luke's phrasing -- "full of the Holy Spirit" and "led by the Spirit into the wilderness" -- sets up the test as a Spirit-empowered confrontation. The wilderness (eremos) recalls Israel's forty years of wilderness testing (Deut 8:2: "to humble and test you"); Jesus' forty days recapitulates and reverses Israel's failures. Jewish apocalyptic tradition associated the wilderness with both divine encounter (Elijah: 1 Kgs 19:8; Moses: Exod 24:18; 34:28) and demonic presence (Isa 34:14; Bar 4:35; 1 Enoch 10).
2: Fasting for forty days connects Jesus to Moses (Exod 34:28: "He was there with the Lord forty days and forty nights; he neither ate bread nor drank water") and Elijah (1 Kgs 19:8). Prolonged fasting was practiced in Second Temple Judaism as a means of intensifying prayer and visionary experience (Dan 10:3; 4 Ezra 5:19-20). "The devil" (diabolos) is the LXX rendering of the Hebrew Satan (ha-satan = the accuser), a figure prominent in Job 1-2, Zech 3:1-2, and 1 Chr 21:1. Luke uses diabolos rather than Satanas here, emphasizing the role of adversarial accuser familiar to Hellenistic readers.
3: The first temptation exploits the vulnerability of genuine hunger. The command "tell this stone to become bread" echoes the wilderness provision crisis of Exod 16 (manna). Jesus' sonship is challenged: "If you are the Son of God" -- the same taunt used at the crucifixion (23:35-39). In Deut 8:3 (which Jesus will cite), Moses frames the manna not as provision secured by human ingenuity but as divine teaching that "man does not live by bread alone." The temptation is to resolve physical need through autonomous power rather than trust.
4: Jesus cites Deut 8:3 from the LXX: "Man shall not live on bread alone." The Deuteronomy context (chs 6-8) is the foundational OT background for all three temptation responses: Deut 6:13 (worship God alone), Deut 6:16 (do not test), and Deut 8:3 (do not live by bread alone). Jesus thus positions himself as the faithful son who succeeds where Israel (also called God's son, Exod 4:22) failed in the wilderness. The omission of "but by every word of God" present in Matt 4:4 and Deut 8:3 in some Lukan manuscripts is a textual variant; most editions retain it.
5: The second temptation shifts to a panoramic vision of all the kingdoms of the world. "In an instant" (en stigme chronou) highlights the supernatural character of the revelation. The motif of a high mountain from which all kingdoms are visible appears in Jewish apocalyptic: Deut 34:1-3 (Moses sees the whole land from Nebo), 4 Ezra 11-12 (eagle vision), and 1 Enoch 18. The idea that the kingdoms of this world are under the devil's authority reflects a strand of Jewish apocalyptic thought that saw the present age as under evil-angelic dominion (cf. Dan 10:13, 20; Eph 6:12; John 12:31).
6: Satan's claim to have been "given" authority over the kingdoms and to give it to whomever he wishes reflects the dualistic cosmology common in Second Temple apocalyptic literature (1 Enoch 6-11; Jub. 48:9-19; Testament of Moses 10; Qumran's dualistic texts like 1QS 3-4). The language of being "handed over" (paradedotai) echoes the Danielic vision of successive kingdoms given by God to earthly rulers. The offer targets the messianic expectation of universal kingship -- the temptation is to grasp the kingdoms through accommodation to evil rather than through the cross.
7: The condition of the temptation -- "if you worship before me" -- is the demand for proskynesis (prostration before a king or deity), the honor gesture of the Hellenistic world. In the Augustan context of the Roman Empire, emperor worship was beginning to require proskynesis. The temptation reflects the real political-religious pressure of the first century: to achieve dominance by adopting the world's methods of power. Jesus' refusal positions him in opposition to every form of political idolatry.
8: Jesus cites Deut 6:13: "Worship the Lord your God and serve him only." The Shema context (Deut 6:4-9, the daily Jewish prayer) is deliberately invoked -- this is not merely a proof text but the foundational confession of Jewish monotheism. "Serve him only" (autō monō latreuseis) uses latreuō, the Greek word for cultic service/worship, the same word used in the LXX for Israel's liturgical worship of God. The application to Satan's offer: his service is not latreia but the abandonment of the one true God.
9: The temple pinnacle (pterygion tou hierou) was probably the southeast corner of the temple mount, where the Royal Stoa overhangs the Kidron Valley -- a drop of some 450 feet according to Josephus (Ant. 15.411-12). This was the highest visible point of the entire temple complex. In rabbinic tradition, a legend circulated that the Messiah would appear on the roof of the temple (j. Berakhot 2:4). The temptation is to perform a spectacular messianic sign that would force God's protective response -- to "test" God by compelling a miracle.
10: Satan cites Ps 91:11-12 (the psalm of divine protection) accurately, demonstrating that Scripture can be weaponized for false purposes. Psalm 91 was widely used in Second Temple Judaism as protection against demonic attack (4QPsAp = 11Q11, an Aramaic Psalms scroll uses this psalm in an exorcism formula). The irony: a psalm designed to protect the faithful from spiritual harm is used by the adversary to provoke presumption.
11: The angels who "will lift you up so that you will not strike your foot against a stone" (Ps 91:12) evokes the tradition of guardian angels. Jewish angelology in the Second Temple period expanded significantly: Gabriel and Michael as archangels (Dan 8:16; 10:13; 1 Enoch 9), guardian angels for individuals (Tob 5:4-16; Matt 18:10), and angelic protection of the righteous (Ps 34:7; Dan 6:22). The temptation to spectacular display exploits the genuine promise of protection to serve self-promotion.
12: Jesus cites Deut 6:16: "Do not put the Lord your God to the test." The Deuteronomy context is the incident at Massah (Exod 17:1-7), where Israel tested God by demanding water: "Is the Lord among us or not?" To test (ekpeirazō) God is to demand that he prove himself on human terms -- a fundamental inversion of faith. "Tempting the Lord" by the spectacular jump would be forcing God to act on a human schedule to prove his protective presence.
13: "Until an opportune time" (achri kairou) is a programmatic marker in Luke. The devil's departure is temporary; Luke explicitly connects Satan's return to the passion: 22:3 ("Satan entered Judas"), 22:31 ("Satan has asked to sift all of you as wheat"), 22:53 ("this is your hour -- when darkness reigns"). The "opportune time" anticipates the full assault of the passion, framing Luke's entire Gospel as a single extended conflict between the Spirit-empowered Jesus and the adversary who retreats only to return at the cross.
14: The return to Galilee "in the power of the Spirit" (en tē dynamei tou pneumatos) transitions from the private temptation ordeal to the public ministry. Galilee in the first century was a prosperous agricultural and fishing region under Herod Antipas's tetrarchy, with a mixed population of Jews and Gentiles on its margins. Its distance from Jerusalem and its reputation as provincial (John 7:52: "no prophet comes from Galilee") makes it an unlikely starting point for messianic activity. The "reports" (phēmē) spreading through the region anticipates the fame-and-withdrawal pattern of the Galilean ministry.
15: Synagogue teaching was the primary vehicle for the spread of Jesus' ministry in Galilee. First-century synagogues served as community centers for study, prayer, legal assembly, and social welfare -- not merely houses of worship. The synagogue structure involved Torah reading (in Hebrew with Aramaic targum interpretation), a Prophets reading (haftarah), a sermon, and prayer. Any qualified adult male could be invited to read and expound. Jesus' teaching role "in their synagogues" (en tais synagogais autōn) is Luke's summary of the Galilee phase before the Nazareth crisis.
17: The Isaiah scroll handed to Jesus was a physical parchment scroll (megillāh). The reader unrolled (anoixas) the scroll to find the desired passage -- without chapter divisions or pagination, requiring familiarity with the text. Luke specifies that Jesus "found the place" where the Isa 61 passage was written, suggesting deliberate selection rather than the assigned lectionary reading. The absence of a fixed haftarah lectionary in the first century (the triennial cycle developed later) means the reader had some freedom in Prophets selection.
18: Isaiah 61:1-2 is the Jubilee charter of Israel's liberation. The Jubilee (Lev 25:8-55) mandated every fiftieth year: release of debts, freeing of slaves, return of alienated land. By the first century, the Jubilee had not been observed for centuries and had taken on eschatological significance -- the great Jubilee that God would inaugurate in the messianic age. The Qumran community used Isa 61 messianically (11QMelchizedek applies it to the eschatological figure Melchizedek). "Good news to the poor" (euangelisasthai ptōchois) uses the same vocabulary as the Isaiah 40 herald whose voice cries in the wilderness (3:4).
19: "The year of the Lord's favor" (eniauton Kyriou dekton) is the Jubilee year of Lev 25:10 ("proclaim liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants"). Isaiah 61:2 adds "the day of vengeance of our God" -- significantly, Jesus stops reading mid-sentence at "year of the Lord's favor" and does not read "the day of vengeance." This deliberate omission has been noted by interpreters since the early church: Jesus' mission is the Jubilee of grace; the judgment element is deferred. The selection and truncation of the text is itself an interpretive act.
20: The hazzan (synagogue attendant) was responsible for the care of the sacred scrolls, the building maintenance, and the logistics of the reading. Rolling the scroll back up (ptuxas) and returning it to the attendant followed standard synagogue protocol. Jesus then "sat down" -- the teacher's posture in Judaism (the seated teacher of authority, as in Matt 5:1; 23:2: "the scribes and Pharisees sit in Moses' seat"). The congregation's gaze (atenizontes) signals the heightened expectation before the exposition begins.
21: "Today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing" -- the programmatic statement of Lukan inaugurated eschatology. Sēmeron (today) is one of Luke's characteristic terms: 2:11 (born today), 4:21 (fulfilled today), 19:5 (today I must stay), 19:9 (today salvation has come), 23:43 (today you will be with me in paradise). The "today" of the kingdom's arrival is not a future expectation but a present reality in the person of Jesus. "In your hearing" (en tois ōsin hymōn) makes the congregation not merely audience but witnesses of fulfillment.
22: The initial response combines admiration (ethaumazon = they marveled) with the words of grace (tois logois tēs charitos). The question "Isn't this Joseph's son?" is the identity challenge. In honor-shame culture, a craftsman's son from a village had no standing to make prophetic claims of fulfillment -- his social location was known and fixed. The question is not necessarily hostile; it reflects bewilderment at the gap between the claim and the known social status. The shift from wonder to fury (v.28) when Jesus invokes the Gentile precedents is the narrative's sharp turn.
23: "Physician, heal yourself" was a known proverb in antiquity. The Talmud preserves a version (b. Bava Kamma 85a). Seneca uses the Greek equivalent (Ep. 27.1). The proverb carries the sense: demonstrate by what you do for yourself what you claim to do for others. Jesus anticipates the challenge: you did great things in Capernaum -- do the same here. The pairing reveals that news of his Capernaum ministry has already reached Nazareth -- the fame of v.14 has preceded him.
24: "No prophet is accepted in his hometown" (en tē patridi autou) is a proverbial form attested also in the Gospel of Thomas (logion 31) and echoed in John 4:44. The proverb reflects a sociological reality of honor-shame cultures: familiarity breeds skepticism; the known social status makes extraordinary claims incredible. Luke's version uses "accepted" (dektos) -- the same word used in v.19 for the "year of the Lord's favor" (eniauton Kyriou dekton). The rejected prophet does not receive the receptivity (dektos) that characterizes God's Jubilee.
25: The Elijah reference (1 Kgs 17-18) is the first of two Gentile-mission precedents Jesus invokes. Elijah's ministry under Ahab included the three-and-a-half-year drought (1 Kgs 18:1 says three years; Luke's three and a half years aligns with an apocalyptic tradition used in Dan 7:25; 12:7; Rev 11:2; 12:14). "Many widows in Israel" -- the contrast is between the abundance of need within Israel and the divine choice to send Elijah to the Phoenician widow of Zarephath (Sidon). Zarephath (modern Sarafand, Lebanon) was pagan territory -- a pointed example of God's provision bypassing Israel.
26: The widow of Zarephath (1 Kgs 17:8-24) was a Phoenician Gentile to whom Elijah was sent during the famine. The Zarephath episode includes the miraculous provision of flour and oil, and the raising of her son from death. By citing this precedent, Jesus implicitly analogizes his own ministry: like Elijah, his most receptive audience may be outside Israel's ethnic boundaries. The Gentile-mission implication is the precipitating cause of the Nazareth crowd's fury (v.28) -- it is not merely hometown pride but the theological scandal of Gentile inclusion that provokes the violent response.
27: Naaman the Syrian (2 Kgs 5:1-14) was a military commander of Aram (Syria) -- not only a Gentile but an enemy of Israel. His leprosy (skin condition) was healed by Elisha's instruction to wash seven times in the Jordan, despite his preference for "better" Syrian rivers. The point Jesus makes: "there were many lepers in Israel in the time of Elisha, and not one of them was cleansed -- only Naaman the Syrian." The divine freedom to heal a foreign enemy rather than Israelite sufferers is the theological principle that makes the Nazareth crowd furious. This anticipates the Gentile centurion (7:9), the Samaritan leper (17:16), and the Gentile mission of Acts.
28: "All the people in the synagogue were furious" (eplēsthēsan ... thumou) -- the transition from wonder to violent fury is rapid. Thymos (fury/rage) is the sudden passion that accompanies honor violation. The crowd has understood what Jesus implies: that the Jubilee he announces will not prioritize Israel, that Gentile outsiders may receive what Nazareth refuses. This is read as a prophetic insult and a rejection of Jewish privilege. The violence that follows (vv.29-30) anticipates the rejection and death that the Gentile-inclusive mission will bring.
29: The brow of the hill on which Nazareth was built -- the topography of ancient Nazareth included a ridge running above the settlement. Tradition identifies the location with the Precipice of Nazareth (modern Jebel Qafza, about 4 km south of the old city), though this identification is disputed; others locate the "brow" at the ridge directly above the ancient village. The attempted mob execution echoes the stoning procedure for blasphemers (Lev 24:14-16; Acts 7:57-58 for Stephen's stoning) with the preliminary casting out of the city before the execution.
30: "He walked right through the crowd and went on his way" -- Jesus' unharmed passage through the mob has been interpreted as miraculous divine protection (cf. John 8:59; 10:39), as a natural result of the crowd's hesitation, or as Luke's theological statement that the hour of his death has not yet come (cf. John 7:30: "his hour had not yet come"). Luke's brevity here is significant: no explanation is given, preserving the mysterious quality of the divine protection. The episode frames the entire Galilean ministry as moving toward a destined ending that cannot be rushed.
31: Capernaum (modern Capernaum / Tell Hum on the northwest shore of the Sea of Galilee) is the base of operations for Jesus' Galilean ministry. Archaeological excavations have uncovered a first-century synagogue site (the later fourth-century limestone synagogue stands atop basalt foundations that may be first-century), a basalt domestic structure venerated as Peter's house by the second century, and commercial fishing installations. The site is on the Via Maris trade route, explaining the presence of a Roman centurion (7:1-10) and a tax office (5:27). Capernaum was prosperous by Galilean village standards.
32: "His teaching amazed them because his message had authority" (exousia) -- first-century synagogue teaching typically involved citing rabbinic authorities (as the Pharisees and scribes did: "Rabbi X said in the name of Rabbi Y..."). Jesus teaches differently: he speaks from his own authority rather than citing predecessors. This is the same quality Matthew notes in the Sermon on the Mount: "You have heard it said... but I say to you" (Matt 5:21-48). The exousia that marks his teaching is the same exousia that commands demons (v.36) and forgives sins (5:24) -- it is the authority of his person, not his credentials.
33: The presence of a person with an "impure spirit" (pneuma daimoniou akathartou) in the synagogue itself is noteworthy -- demon possession was not confined to social margins in first-century Jewish understanding. Second Temple Judaism had a developed demonology: Asmodeus (Tobit), Mastema (Jubilees), Belial (DSS), Azazel (1 Enoch). Exorcism was practiced by Jewish exorcists (Acts 19:13-16; Josephus Ant. 8.46-49 describes Solomon-tradition exorcism). The synagogue setting makes this a public, official confrontation -- the unclean spirit is present at the reading of Torah, making the contrast with Jesus' "holy" identity stark.
34: "What do you want with us, Jesus of Nazareth?" -- the plural "us" in demon speech may reflect the plural nature of possession (cf. 8:30, Legion), the solidarity of the demonic realm, or an inclusive address on behalf of all unclean spirits threatened by Jesus' presence. "The Holy One of God" (ho hagios tou theou) is a specific title: in the OT it is used of the consecrated priest (Ps 106:16, of Aaron) and of the divine warrior; in early Christian usage it becomes a messianic title (John 6:69; Acts 3:14). The demon's recognition of Jesus' identity before any human confession is a Markan-Lukan motif of ironic disclosure.
35: Jesus' exorcism command -- "Be quiet! Come out of him!" (phimōthēti kai exelthe) -- uses the same verb (phimoō = muzzle, silence) used of silencing unruly animals. The demon's convulsive exit (throwing the man down in the middle without harming him) distinguishes Jesus' exorcism from typical ancient exorcism, which could be violent and prolonged. Jewish exorcism practices described by Josephus (Ant. 8.47: ring with Solomon's seal, incantation texts, burning roots) and the DSS Prayer of Nabonidus contrast with Jesus' immediate, word-only exorcism -- no ritual paraphernalia, no prolonged formula.
36: "What words these are! With authority and power he gives orders to impure spirits and they come out" -- Luke's crowd distinguishes two qualities: logos (word/authority) and dynamis (power). The pairing of exousia (authority/legal right) and dynamis (miraculous power) appears again in 4:14 and Luke 9:1 (when Jesus sends the Twelve with "power and authority over all demons"). In Hellenistic philosophy, a word (logos) with real power (dynamis) to effect change was the mark of a divine being. The crowd's astonishment is not merely at the exorcism but at the mode: command alone, instantaneous compliance.
37: "Reports about him spread throughout the surrounding area" -- the Galilee region encompassed the cities of the Decapolis to the east, Phoenician territory to the north, Samaria to the south, and Gaulanitis (the Golan) to the northeast. The fame narrative in Luke (5:15; 7:17) contrasts with Jesus' frequent attempts at discretion (4:41; 5:14) -- the healing ministry generates public attention he does not seek. Josephus describes Galilee as densely populated (Life 235: "208 villages and towns"), making rapid rumor-spread through the region plausible.
38: The house of Simon (Peter) has been associated archaeologically with a first-century domestic structure at Capernaum venerated as early as the second century CE. The structure shows evidence of repeated plaster applications and graffiti that include the name "Peter" in various languages, suggesting continuous Christian veneration (later covered by a Byzantine octagonal church). That Peter is married is confirmed by 1 Cor 9:5 ("a believing wife, as do the other apostles and the Lord's brothers and Cephas"). First-century Galilean houses were typically single or two-room structures with a central courtyard shared by an extended family group.
39: "He bent over her and rebuked the fever" -- Luke, as a physician (Col 4:14), uses the medical-adjacent term "high fever" (pyretos megas) -- the only NT writer to specify the intensity of fever. The rebuking of a fever as if it were a demonic entity (the same verb epitimaō used in v.35 for the demon) reflects the ancient understanding that diseases could be caused by demonic affliction (cf. 13:16: the bent woman "whom Satan has kept bound"). The immediate effect -- she got up and began to serve them -- demonstrates complete recovery, as fevers typically leave a period of weakness. Luke's medical eye notes the completeness of the cure.
40: "As the sun was setting" -- the end of Sabbath (at sunset Saturday), when the Sabbath restrictions on carrying and travel lifted, the people brought those who were sick. The Sabbath technically ended when three stars were visible (m. Shabbat 2:7); until then, carrying the sick to Jesus would violate the Sabbath work prohibition. The gathering of the whole town at Simon's door mirrors the synagogue assembly of the morning -- Capernaum has been electrified by the day's events. The individual healing through laying on of hands (epititheis tas cheiras) was a recognized gesture of blessing and healing in both Jewish (Gen 48:14) and early Christian practice (Acts 6:6; 8:17).
41: The demons' confessional cry "You are the Son of God" (Huios tou theou) is silenced by Jesus because they "knew he was the Messiah" (Christon auton einai). This Messianic Secret motif (prominent in Mark) appears in Luke as selective suppression: demons may know Jesus' identity, but their testimony is not the appropriate vehicle for his self-disclosure. The contrast is with human witnesses like Peter (9:20) and the centurion (23:47) whose confessions are received. In Second Temple Judaism, divine sonship was used of Israel as a whole (Exod 4:22; Hos 11:1), of the Davidic king (2 Sam 7:14; Ps 2:7), and of righteous individuals (Wis 2:18) -- the demonic recognition goes beyond any of these to identify the specific person of Jesus.
42: Jesus' withdrawal to a solitary place for prayer is a Lukan pattern (3:21; 5:16; 6:12; 9:18, 28; 11:1; 22:41). Luke adds prayer to several episodes where Mark/Matthew have none, reflecting his theological emphasis on Jesus' Spirit-sustained relationship with the Father. The crowd's attempt to keep him from leaving (katechoō = hold back, restrain) reflects the Galilean enthusiasm that Jesus consistently resists -- his ministry is not captive to any one community's needs or expectations. The scene anticipates the pattern of rejection and departure that characterizes the Galilean ministry.
43: "I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God" (dei me euangelisasthai) -- dei (it is necessary, I must) is one of Luke's characteristic divine-necessity terms, appearing at key junctures: 2:49 (I must be in my Father's house), 4:43 (I must preach), 9:22 (the Son of Man must suffer), 13:33 (a prophet must die in Jerusalem), 22:37 (this must be fulfilled). The dei frames Jesus' ministry as divinely commissioned necessity rather than human preference. The scope -- "to the other towns also" -- drives the mission outward in exactly the direction Nazareth has refused (vv.23-27).
44: Luke's summary of the synagogue preaching circuit in "Judea" (some manuscripts read "Galilee," which matches the geographic context; "Judea" in Luke can refer to the whole Jewish land rather than just the southern province, cf. 1:5; 6:17; 7:17; 23:5). The synagogue circuit was a natural infrastructure for itinerant teaching: each town's synagogue provided a venue, an audience, and a social network. The summary closes the Nazareth-Capernaum unit and opens the way for the disciple-call narrative (ch5), which will give the mission its ongoing human form.
